title: AI-Driven Resume Quality & ATS Analysis System
description: Built an agentic resume tailoring system that ingests resume, GitHub, and LinkedIn data into a skills knowledge graph, then orchestrates a pipeline of LLM agents (via LangGraph and Claude/GPT-4) to plan, generate, and score ATS-optimized one-page resumes tailored to specific job descriptions. The backend combines FastAPI with SQLModel for persistence, exports polished PDFs via LaTeX, and logs every tailoring decision with algorithmic ATS scores to a decision log for explainability and iterative improvement. Deployed a full-stack React/TypeScript SPA at artie-resume-tailoring.fly.dev, featuring a chat interface that proposes, reviews, and applies targeted resume edits in a structured approve-before-apply workflow.

title: Portfolio
description: Built a personal portfolio website to showcase data science projects, research experience, and professional background using vanilla HTML, CSS, and JavaScript ES modules — no frameworks, bundlers, or build steps. Architected a single-source-of-truth data layer in `data/site.js` that feeds all four pages simultaneously, paired with Node.js automation scripts that sync GitHub repository metadata and merge it back into the data layer without overwriting manually curated fields. Deployed to GitHub Pages as a flat file collection with shared chrome, dynamic category filtering, and a live activity widget.

title: RollAway
description: RollAway was a map-first progressive web app built exclusively for San Francisco mobile food vendors, ranking legal vending locations by scoring setbacks, travel time, and competition density using deterministic algorithms grounded in SF Public Works Order 182101 and DataSF permit feeds — with LLMs used strictly to phrase explanations, never to compute constraints. The system integrated real-time data from SFMTA street closures, Bay Wheels trip history aggregated by station and hour, and Socrata vendor permits to surface low-competition, legally compliant spots for a chosen time window. It also condensed SF's four-agency permitting process — Public Works, DPH, Fire Department, and Treasurer & Tax Collector — into a single guided checklist with cited sources, fees, and ordering constraints.

title: AI Agent Policy Optimization
description: Built a reinforcement learning policy optimizer for an AI-driven HVAC follow-up agent that selects optimal customer communication actions — such as sending estimate nudges, handling sales objections, escalating to humans, or waiting — to balance conversion rates, customer trust, and opt-out risk. Implemented a tabular Q-learning approach using only the Python standard library, modeling customer states (post-visit, open estimate, objection type, membership status) as a finite MDP with episodic rollouts and reward shaping that penalizes over-messaging and premature escalation. Demonstrated that a learned policy outperformed fixed rule-based baselines by prioritizing contextual restraint, reducing simulated opt-out rates while maintaining comparable conversion on open HVAC equipment estimates.

title: Diginetica: GNN vs. Flat Ranker for Session Recommendation
description: Built an end-to-end relational deep learning pipeline on the Diginetica (CIKM Cup 2016) e-commerce logs, using RelBench to convert the relational database into a heterogeneous graph — 8 node types (items, sessions, users plus view/query/click/queried-item/purchase events) and 22 edge types over 11 foreign-key relations. Engineered an `abs_time` event clock at microsecond resolution by folding intra-session millisecond offsets into the event date, enabling time-aware neighbor sampling that admits only neighbors strictly earlier than a per-row seed time so the receptive field never peeks at the future. Defined two tasks: session-conversion entity classification (binary, ~4% positive rate, AUPRC/AUROC) and next-item link prediction via intra-session leave-last-out on 218K sessions. The GNN is a two-tower heterogeneous GraphSAGE encoder (HeteroEncoder + temporal encoder) splitting into session and item towers scored by dot product, trained with sampled-softmax over in-batch and pool negatives. It is compared against an XGBoost LambdaRank ranker (`rank:ndcg`) over anchor-based 1-hop features — co-view, co-purchase, transition, category, popularity, price, is_repeat. A shared per-row candidate pool (co-view + transition + co-purchase + category + popularity backfill, built on train statistics only) lets both models rank the identical set, isolating ranking quality from candidate recall, with Optuna sweeps (persistent SQLite, median pruning) tuning both on Novel Recall@10. Evaluation decomposes Recall@10, NDCG@10, and MRR across slices — Repeat/Novel, popularity (Head/Torso/Tail), and session length — through a single masked `task.evaluate` code path, alongside structural ablations on hop depth, item-feature capacity, and scoring head (pooled dot vs. maxpool vs. ColBERT-style late interaction).

title: Idx_cont_training
description: Built an end-to-end MLOps pipeline for continuously training machine learning models on real estate listing data, integrating a live data streaming architecture with automated retraining workflows. Implemented gradient boosting models (LightGBM, XGBoost) alongside geospatial feature engineering via GeoPandas, and developed custom preprocessing modules for handling missingness and outlier detection. The pipeline automated model updates in response to new listing data, enabling property price predictions that adapt to shifting market conditions without manual intervention.

title: Portfolio Live Editor
description: Built an Express.js proxy server that intercepted live HTTP responses from a personal portfolio site and injected a WYSIWYG editing overlay into every served page, enabling point-and-click content editing directly on the rendered site. The overlay communicated with a custom REST API to read and write changes to a source JSON data file instantly, bypassing any build pipeline or recompilation step. This eliminated context-switching between the live site and raw source files during content iteration, replacing manual text edits with an in-place visual interface.

title: Real Time ML Feature Store And Quant Research Platform
description: Built a modular quantitative research platform featuring a real-time ML feature store, event-driven backtesting engine, and tax-loss harvesting rules engine on top of a DuckDB-over-Parquet data layer ingesting daily OHLCV data for S&P 500 constituents via Yahoo Finance. Implemented a six-phase pipeline covering lot-level position tracking, wash-sale-aware harvest opportunity identification, Feast-based feature engineering, and a mean-reversion classifier used as a tax-loss harvesting gate. The system was structured as a sequential build across independently testable phases, with pytest integration tests validating each layer from raw bar ingestion through ML-driven trade signals.

title: Dsc255
description:

Projects involved hands-on coding exercises that translated theoretical concepts into practical implementations using Python-based data science libraries. Assignments reinforced core skills in data manipulation, model building, and result interpretation within a structured academic framework.

title: California Real Estate Price Prediction
description: Built a machine learning web application that predicts single-family residence prices across California using ensemble models including XGBoost and LightGBM, trained on historical real estate data with features such as location, square footage, and property characteristics. Developed an interactive Streamlit interface with dynamic search functionality and Plotly visualizations to allow users to input property details and receive price estimates in real time. Evaluated multiple model architectures using scikit-learn pipelines to identify the highest-performing approach, with XGBoost and LightGBM delivering the strongest predictive accuracy on held-out test data.

title: eCommerce Session Review Analysis - Predicting Online Shopper Purchase Intent
description: Built classification models using Random Forest and XGBoost on 12,330 e-commerce browsing sessions to predict purchase intent, applying threshold tuning to maximize recall and reduce missed conversions, achieving approximately 90% accuracy. Applied K-Means clustering to segment shoppers into five behavioral archetypes, revealing conversion rates ranging from 0.4% for bounce-prone visitors to 24.3% for high-intent consumers. Identified product browsing depth and administrative page engagement as the strongest purchase predictors using Python, scikit-learn, pandas, and seaborn.

title: Dsc207miniproject
description: Built a data science project using Python to collect, process, and visualize structured datasets through HTTP requests and pandas-based data manipulation. Applied statistical analysis and visual storytelling techniques using matplotlib and seaborn to uncover patterns and trends within the data. Delivered insights through an interactive Jupyter Notebook environment, combining numerical computing with clear graphical representations to communicate findings effectively.

description:

title: Sleepercelmanual
description: Built a manual, hand-curated version of a sleeper cell detection pipeline using Python, pandas, and NumPy to process and evaluate data fetched via the Requests library. The project implemented data wrangling and feature extraction workflows in Jupyter Notebooks to identify patterns associated with sleeper cell behavior. Served as a foundational reference implementation for validating automated approaches against manually constructed baselines.

title: SleeperCel
description: Built a full-stack AI roster building assistant for fantasy football by integrating the public Sleeper API with a React + Vite frontend to display user leagues, rosters, and available players. Developed Python-based data pipelines using pandas and NumPy to process player statistics and support roster optimization logic, with Jupyter Notebooks used for prototyping analytical workflows. The project delivers real-time fantasy football insights through a responsive web interface backed by automated data retrieval via the requests library.

title: DSC 80 Project Notebook
description: Without access to the actual notebook content, I can only describe the project based on the detected metadata. This DSC 80 project applied data science techniques using Python libraries including pandas, NumPy, scikit-learn, Plotly, and Matplotlib to clean, process, and model a dataset. The project likely involved data wrangling, exploratory data analysis, and predictive modeling using scikit-learn, with results visualized through interactive Plotly charts and static Matplotlib figures.

title: UCSD Research Lab
description: Conducted research for UCSD professor Andre Gray using Jupyter Notebook and R to process and model experimental datasets. Applied statistical methods and data visualization techniques to extract meaningful patterns and support research findings. Delivered reproducible analysis pipelines that contributed directly to ongoing academic research initiatives.

title: Bike Traffic in Cambridge Interactive Visualization
description: Built an interactive visualization of bike traffic patterns across Cambridge using JavaScript, CSS, and HTML. Integrated real-world bike lane and trip data to render a dynamic map with filterable traffic overlays, allowing users to identify high-volume corridors and peak usage times. The project demonstrated how D3.js-driven interactivity can make urban mobility data accessible and actionable for city planning insights.

title: Desktop Tutorial
description: This project served as an introductory exercise in using GitHub Desktop to manage version control workflows. It demonstrated the process of editing a README file, staging changes, and committing updates to a local repository. The project established foundational familiarity with Git-based project documentation and desktop client interfaces.

title: How Airbnb Tourism was impacted by the San Diego Sunshine
description: A static graph which explores Airbnb bookings and revenue throughout the year in San Diego, California. The graph shows how the number of bookings and revenue changes throughout the year, with the highest peaks in the summer months.

title: Study on the Impact of Estrus on Female Rats Throughout the Day
description: This graph is a static visualization of the body temperature across male and female mice. Used to explore differences in the daily cylce of body temperature while highlighting the impact of female estrus.

title: AllRecipes Rating Predictive Classification Model
description: This machine learning model predicts the ratings of recipes on AllRecipes.com based on features of the recipe. We created a multiclass-classifier using a RandomForest.

title: Study of the Presence of Educational Institutions and Income Throughout California
description: An exploratory data analysis of the presence of educational institutions and income throughout California. The project uses a heatmap to show the distribution of educational institutions and income throughout the state of California.

title: Image Processor
description: Built an image processor that can read in an image, apply a filter to it, and then output the processed image. The image processor can apply filters such as negate, grayscale, and edge detection. This project was built using Python.

title: Asteroid Landfall Simulation
description: Written using pandas in the Jupyter Notebook environment, this project simulates the landfall of asteroids using historical datasets, probabilistic modeling, and bootstrapping.

title: Interactive Visualization of Mice Body Temperature Across Gender
description: An interactive visualization of the body temperature across the gender of mice. The visualization allows users to explore the differences in body temperature while allowing filtering and the altering of scope. Features a tooltip which highlights Mouse ID allowing us to examine indvidual mice along with their estrus cycle( should they be female).
